title: How will AI and the fertility crisis interact? - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: In a Free Press column excerpt, Tyler Cowen speculates that as AI absorbs most informational functions and population density falls due to the fertility crisis, in-person human interaction will become scarcer and more valued, pushing people to compete for attention via appearance, charisma, and physical presence — citing 'looksmaxxing' YouTuber Clavicular and rising demand for plastic surgery and GLP-1-successor drugs as early signals.

themes.theme: AI hollowing out human information functions

insights.claim: Tyler Cowen predicts that as AI takes over most informational exchange, each individual encountered in person will be seen as 'something special' simply because in-person human interaction becomes comparatively rare against the constant option of talking to AI.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Implies a future scarcity value placed on human attention and presence as AI commoditizes information exchange — relevant for thinking about experience-economy and in-person service demand.
insights.quote: Each individual will be seen as something special by the other humans.

insights.claim: Cowen argues that falling population density from the fertility crisis will empty out public spaces, causing today's large cities to develop small-town-style social norms — such as greeting strangers on the sidewalk — that are currently rare in dense urban settings.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Connects the fertility decline directly to a prediction about urban social texture — a testable claim about future city life relevant to urban-planning and consumer-behavior forecasts.
insights.quote: Public spaces will be emptier, so anyone out in public will attract more notice.

insights.claim: Cowen expects AI smart glasses to communicate a person's unique identity or backstory to others during in-person social interactions, functioning as an ambient reputation and context layer for human encounters.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Points to a specific product category (AI-mediated social context/reputation glasses) as a plausible near-term consumer AI application worth tracking.
insights.quote: having some kind of unique identity that either is visible upon meeting or which AI smart glasses will communicate during social interactions

insights.claim: Cowen predicts that because AI will perform most informational functions, humans wanting to draw attention will increasingly specialize in the 'remaining human functions' — appearance, charisma, physical touch, and distinctive personal identity — rather than knowledge or information provision.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A structural thesis about where human economic and social value concentrates in an AI-saturated world — favors industries built around appearance, presence, and physical charisma over information services.
insights.quote: you will have to specialize in the remaining human functions.
insights.locator: Many of these humans will invest heavily

insights.claim: Cowen cites YouTube personality 'Clavicular,' known for heavy 'looksmaxxing' including plastic surgery despite having 'achieved nothing of note,' as an early harbinger of a future where appearance-based recognizability substitutes for accomplishment as a source of social attention.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Names a concrete current example treated as a leading indicator of a broader appearance-optimization trend, relevant to tracking consumer demand in cosmetic and aesthetic markets.
insights.quote: Clavicular has achieved nothing of note, except for being immediately recognizable for how he looks.

insights.claim: Cowen predicts that plastic surgery and the successor drugs to GLP-1 weight-loss medications are likely to command even more consumer interest than they do today, as appearance investment becomes a primary way to stand out in an AI-saturated social environment.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A direct, actionable demand forecast for cosmetic-surgery and next-generation weight-loss/appearance-drug markets tied explicitly to the AI adoption curve.
insights.quote: Plastic surgery and the successor drugs to GLP-1s are likely to command even more interest than today.
insights.locator: Clavicular has achieved nothing of note

insights.claim: Cowen frames the future of ordinary social interaction as converging toward 'a gala,' where everyone shows up wanting to look a very particular way to draw attention away from competing with AI for notice.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A compact mental model — 'social life as gala' — for thinking about how AI-driven information abundance reshapes everyday self-presentation norms.
insights.quote: A lot of ordinary social interactions will become more like a gala, where everyone shows up wanting to look a very particular way to draw attention.
insights.locator: If a person comes across as extremely nondescript

themes.theme: Framing this as inevitable, not dystopian

insights.claim: Cowen acknowledges his forecast sounds 'stupid, undesirable, and ridiculous' to people today but argues this mirrors how medieval people would have viewed modern levels of appearance-consciousness, implying the shift will feel normalized in hindsight rather than resisted.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Signals that Cowen expects this appearance-driven social future to be adopted gradually and accepted rather than reversed, a framing relevant to how quickly related consumer trends might scale.
insights.quote: people today care much more about how they look, and can do much more about it, than could people in medieval times.
